coredata.dc:title: Highly-interspersed biomass-derived carbon quantum dots onto floral CoAl-LDH for significantly enhanced CO2 photoreduction into CO and CH4
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jia
Gao, Ge
Liu, Yang
Li, Yang
Liu, Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Stimulating photocatalytic activity of layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have been being as a research hotspot in photocatalysis. Quantum dots doping is a well acknowledged strategy to utilize their photoelectric superiorities to overcome the intrinsic defects of pristine LDHs. In this work, waste biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs)-interspersed CoAl-LDH photocatalysts (B/CALs) are successfully prepared by means of a simple hydrothermal method from green and renewable points of view. The introduction of B-CQDs significantly broadens the light absorption range and capacity of CoAl-LDH. As the terminal electron acceptor, the highly-dispersed B-CQDs can effectively prolong the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. No assistance from any sacrificial agent, the optimal sample of B/CAL-2 exhibits significantly enhanced photoreduction activity of CO2-to-CO and CH4, 6.4- and 7.5-fold higher than those of the pristine CoAl-LDH, respectively, and also surpassing many reported high-level LDHs counterparts. Such an uncomplicated case guides a green and feasible route to modify semiconductor materials with renewable biomass, which is expected to be applied to others for enhanced photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CoAl-LDH
Biomass
Carbon quantum dots
CO2 photoreduction

Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We greatly acknowledge the financial support to this work through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871124 ), the Start-up grant under 130–09419024 from Shantou University (Shantou, China), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province , China ( 2021A1515010071 ), and Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry (Jilin Normal University), Ministry of Education of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the B/CALs (a-d) and EDS of B/CAL-2 (e).
TEM (a and b), HRTEM (c), and HAADF-STEM images with elemental mappings of the B/CAL-2 (d).
AFM images, derived height profiles, and 3D models of the CAL (a, b, c) and B/CAL-2 (d, e, f), N2- (g) and CO2 (h)-sorptions of the CAL and B /CAL-2.
XPS spectra of the CAL and B/CAL-2: full survey spectrum (a), Co 1 s (b), Al 2p (c), C 1 s (d), FT-IR spectra of B-CQDs, CAL, and B/CAL-2 (e).

UV–vis DRS spectra and estimated band gap of CAL (a) and B/CAL-2 (b), valence band XPS measurement of CAL (c), up-converted PL spectra of B-CQDs with varied excitation wavelengths (d), transient photocurrents (e) and EIS (f) of the B-CQDs, CAL, and B /CAL-2.
Yields of CO (a) and CH4 (b) of different samples, H2 yield of the B/CAL-2 (c), and 13C isotopic tracing experiment of the B/CAL-2 (d).
Proposed visible-light CO2 photoreduction mechanism over the B/CAL-2.

head.ce:title: Highly-interspersed biomass-derived carbon quantum dots onto floral CoAl-LDH for significantly enhanced CO2 photoreduction into CO and CH4

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Siping, Jilin 136000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Siping, Jilin, 136000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou, Guangdong 515063, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou, Guangdong, 515063, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimulating photocatalytic activity of layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have been being as a research hotspot in photocatalysis. Quantum dots doping is a well acknowledged strategy to utilize their photoelectric superiorities to overcome the intrinsic defects of pristine LDHs. In this work, waste biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs)-interspersed CoAl-LDH photocatalysts (B/CALs) are successfully prepared by means of a simple hydrothermal method from green and renewable points of view. The introduction of B-CQDs significantly broadens the light absorption range and capacity of CoAl-LDH. As the terminal electron acceptor, the highly-dispersed B-CQDs can effectively prolong the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. No assistance from any sacrificial agent, the optimal sample of B/CAL-2 exhibits significantly enhanced photoreduction activity of CO2-to-CO and CH4, 6.4- and 7.5-fold higher than those of the pristine CoAl-LDH, respectively, and also surpassing many reported high-level LDHs counterparts. Such an uncomplicated case guides a green and feasible route to modify semiconductor materials with renewable biomass, which is expected to be applied to others for enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots are highly interspersed onto floral CoAl-LDH by a simple yet effective hydrothermal method, which exhibits prominent photocatalytic activity of CO2-to-CO and CH4 under visible-light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, carbon dioxide (CO2) photoreduction with water has been being considering as a promising way for sustainable development of human community because it can not only effectively recycle carbon source to high value-added C1 and/or C2 clean fuels, but it also significantly alleviate the increasing conflict between the tremendous consumption of carbon-rich fossil fuel and overwhelming emission of CO2 for harmful greenhouse effect [1–3]. Unfortunately, the dissociation of CO in CO2 molecules requires an ultra-high activation energy up to ∼750 kJ mol−1 [4–6]. Therefore, exploring suitable photocatalysts with towering merits of wide and sufficient light absorption, high charge separation efficiency, and strong adsorption and activation of CO2 have being highly challenging and appealing to facilitate CO2 photoreduction and achieve satisfactory performance [7–9].

Due to the strong light harvestability and photostability, good electronic conductivity and water solubility, small geometric dimensions with high dispersion, multiple exciton generation, and fabulous bulk-to-surface transferability of photogenerated electrons, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been testified to be as excellent additives to improve the photocatalytic activity for pristine semiconductors [21,22]. In comparison with the traditional CQDs prepared from the fussy and non-green chemical synthesis, recent attentions are been focusing on using various renewable biomasses as carbon sources to prepare biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) by simplified synthetic procedure, which have been highly interspersed on CuS [23], Bi2WO6 [24,25], TiO2 [26], Bi2MoO6/Bi2S3 [27], and Bi2WO6/Cu2O [28] for enhanced photocatalytic activities of organic pollutant degradations, respectively. In these work, the B-CQDs can act as an adsorbent to increase adsorption capacity towards the target pollutants. More importantly, the photoexcited electrons will be quickly transferred through them rather than aggregate on the photocatalysts. As a result, the recombination tendency of the photogenerated electrons and holes on the photocatalysts will be greatly impeded and the lifetime of photogenerated electrons will be prolonged remarkably. Therefore, it seems to be a nice recipe to further ameliorate the photoelectric properties of LDHs with B-CQDs to fulfill superior CO2 photoreduction activity.

Evoked by this interest, herein, we utilize a waste biomass of withered magnolia as carbon source to prepare a B-CQDs-interspersed CoAl-LDH (B/CAL) photocatalyst for CO2 photoreduction via a simple hydrothermal route. Without addition of any sacrificial agent during reaction, the resulting B/CAL exhibits significantly enhanced activity than the pristine CoAl-LDH. The possible migration pathways of photogenerated charge carriers for CO2 photoreduction are systematically investigated by various characterization techniques. This work offers a ‘changing waste into wealth’ idea to arouse the photocatalytic activity of LDH with CQDs and deserves to be further implemented to more pristine semiconductors or heterojunction photocatalysts for broader photocatalytic applications.

The withered magnolia blossoms used in this work were collected from the campus of Shantou University during March to April, 2022. Co(NO3)20.6 H2O (98%), Al(NO3)30.9 H2O (98%), NH4F (GR), Urea (AR), KBr (AR), and BaSO4 (AR) were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ethanol (C2H5OH, 95%), oleic acid (AR), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Firstly, the withered magnolia blossoms were washed by deionized water repeatedly and then dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 10 h. Next, the dried sample was fully smashed in a mortar to be powdery. 1.0 g of the as-prepared powder was dispersed into a mixed solution of 20 mL deionized water and 20 mL ethanol, then the resulting suspension was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steels autoclave, which was heated to 180 °C and maintained it for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were dialyzed for 24 h, and the obtained solution was dried in a freeze dryer for 48 h to obtain B-CQDs.

Briefly, 0.1 g of as-prepared B-CQDs, 6.0 mmol of Co(NO3)20.6 H2O, 2.0 mmol of Al(NO3)30.9 H2O, 40.0 mmol of urea, and 10.0 mmol of NH4F were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and vigorously stirred for 30 min. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave to conduct a hydrothermal process at 120 °C for 24 h. The product was washed with deionized water repeatedly until the pH of solution reach neutrality. After drying at 60 °C, the B-CQDs-interspersed CoAl-LDH was obtained and denoted as B/CAL-2. For comparison, other two B/CAL samples were also prepared under the identical procedure as that for the B/CAL-2 with the varied amount of added B-CQDs (0.05 g, and 0.2 g) and denoted as B/CAL-1 and B/CAL-3, respectively. In addition, the pristine CoAl-LDH was prepared without addition of B-CQDs and denoted as CAL.

0.05 g of sample was put into 100 mL deionized water by ultrasonication for 5 min. After sealing the quartz reactor, CO2 was passed through for 15 min to degas residual air from the solution. Then, the reaction mixture was continuously irradiated under visible light by a 300 W Xe-lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter at a distance of 15 cm, and no any sacrificial reagent is used. At an interval of 1 h, 500 μL gaseous products from the reaction were sampled and analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-7920) with a thermal conductive detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID). The digital images of reaction instruments are illustrated in Fig. S1. The presented activity data are the mean values of three evaluations.
The characterization details and photoelectrochemical measurements of samples are provided in Supporting information.

The crystalline structures of the different samples are initially investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1. The B-CQDs displays a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = ∼22°, indicative of characteristic of amorphous carbon, which is well consistent with the previous report for CQDs [29]. For the CAL prepared without addition of B-CQDs, seven typical diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11°, 23°, 34°, 39°, 46°, 60° and 62° can be found, corresponding to (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110) and (113) lattice planes of CoAl-LDH [30], respectively. The B/CALs prepared with the addition of varied amount B-CQDs display the accordant positions of diffraction peaks as those of the CAL, except that the intensities present strengthening or weakening trend, which is likely due to the interaction of CoAl-LDHs with B-CQDs, restraining or favoring the stacking and growth of the different lattice planes of CoAl-LDH. Moreover, all the diffraction peaks of B/CALs shift to a lower angle, further disclosing the existence of interaction between the CoAl-LDH and B-CQDs. In addition, no diffraction peaks of amorphous carbon are detectable in the B/CALs due to either the low loading content of B-CQDs or its high dispersion on the CoAl-LDH.

The morphology and microstructure of different samples are observed by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the morphology of the CAL and B/CALs differ from sample to sample. The CAL presents typical three-dimensional (3D) petals-like microspheres consisting of a great deal of ultra-thin nanosheets with compact alignment (Fig. 2a). As the B-CQDs are added, the nanosheet thickness of B/CAL-1 increase with the decaying tendency of petals-like microspheres (Fig. 2b), and further addition of B-CQDs exacerbates the architecture deformation of B/CAL-2 (Fig. 2d). When the added amount of B-CQDs is finally increased to 0.2 g, the petals-like microspheres almost collapse with rambling, incompact, irregular, and stacking lamella (Fig. 2c). This is stemmed possibly from the overwhelming addition of B-CQDs into the precursors during the preparation of B/CALs, which changes the growth microenvironment for the petals-like microspheres of resulting CoAl-LDH nanosheets. The corresponding EDS spectroscopy of B/CAL-2 further solidifies the coexistence of Co, Al, O, and C elements (Fig. 2e). As seen from Fig. S2a, the CAL exhibits regular 2D hexagonal nanosheets with a smooth surface exfoliating from the 3D petals-like microspheres. Fig. S2b depicts the good preparation of the B-CQDs with an average size of ∼2.5 nm. Due to the introduction of B-CQDs, the B/CAL-2 shows irregular deformation of the hexagonal nanosheets with obvious overlaps and serious surface roughness, coinciding with that observed from SEM image, as well as implying its potential higher specific surface area (SSA) than the CAL, which is favorable to adsorbing more CO2 molecules ( Fig. 3a). In a partial magnified image (Fig. 3b), the B-CQDs are highly-interspersed in the CAL nanosheets without any aggregation into large clusters, as seen by the tiny, spherical, and black spots. Further HRTEM investigation of the B/CAL-2 (Fig. 3c) reveals that the measurable interplanar distance is 0.268 nm, ascribed to the (012) crystal plane of CoAl-LDH. The interplanar distance for the interspersed B-CQDs in CoAl-LDH is 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 3c), indicative of the (100) crystal phase of graphite carbon. Moreover, the elemental mappings derived from the HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 3d) in the selected marginal region of nanosheets exhibit that the constitutive elementals of Co, Al, O, and C are evenly distributed in the nanosheets. All of these observations confirm that the B-CQDs are successfully incorporated into the CoAl-LDH, which are in good line with the XRD results.

The surface roughness of CAL and B/CAL-2 are further measured by the AFM, as shown in Fig. 4a and d. After the fitted calculation, the average thickness of the selected region for the CAL is less than 8 nm (Fig. 4b), while the one for the B/CAL-2 is almost to 13 nm (Fig. 4e). Based on the 3D images and simulation (Fig. 4c and f), the calculated roughness of B/CAL-2 (Rb = 6.64) is higher than that of the CAL (Rb = 4.76), approving the presumption from TEM observation. Evidently, the introduction of B-CQDs into the CAL remarkably increases its roughness, further indicating the B-CQDs have an important effect on regulating the nucleation and growth of the resulting CoAl-LDH nanosheets. It is commonly accepted amongst photocatalysts that a higher surface roughness corresponds to a larger specific surface area (SSA) to provide more active sites and CO2 adsorption capacity. To shed light on these, both N2- and CO2-sorptions are carried out to assess the SSA and CO2 adsorption capacity of the CAL and B/CAL-2. As shown in Fig. 4g, two samples present II-isotherm with type-H3 hysteresis loop, lacking of an adsorption plateau at the P/P0 = 1.0. The B/CAL-2 possesses a larger area of hysteresis loop with an enhanced adsorbed volume between the P/P0 of 0.8–1.0 than the CAL (inset), suggesting that its pore configuration is a rigidly-connected collection of loosely coherent particles with both mesoporosity and macroporosity [31]. This is originated from the highly-interspersed B-CQDs on surface of CoAl-LDH. Based on the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda method, the calculated SSA of the B/CAL-2 is 56.8 m2 g−1, close twice as much that of CAL (28.9 m2 g−1). The CO2-sorption profiles of the B/CAL-2 also exhibit the more absorbed amounts of CO2 than the CAL (Fig. 4h), coinciding well with the results from N2-sorption. This means the textual properties of CAL has changed due to the introduction of B-CQDs for the preparation of B/CAL-2, and more CO2 molecules can absorbed on the B/CAL-2 with strong interaction for enhanced photoreduction activity.

XPS analysis is further conducted to obtain the surface chemical composition of CAL and B/CAL-2, as shown in Fig. 5. The full survey spectrum in Fig. 5a displays the constitutive Al, C, O, Co elements without other impurities for the two samples. The detected weak signal of C 1 s in the CAL comes from the interlayer CO3 2- anions of CoAl-LDH, which is originated from the added urea during the preparation. For the B/CAL-2, the peak intensity of C 1 s is much higher than that of the CAL, further elucidating the successful introduction of B-CQDs into the CAL and the strengthened signal response of XPS for the resulting B/CAL-2. Fig. 5b-d depicts the high resolution of Co 2p, Al 2p, and C 1 s spectra of the two samples, respectively. For the CAL, the Co 2p spectrum can be divided into two pairs of typical characteristic peaks at 781.3 and 797. 6 eV for Co 2p3/2, 783.0 and 803.3 eV for Co 2p1/2, and a satellite peak at 786.8 eV (Fig. 5b), suggesting the coexistence of Co3+ and Co2+ [32]. In Fig. 5c, the characteristic peak of Al 2p spectrum center at 74.5 eV, correlated to the Al-OH in the interlayer space of CoAl-LDH [33]. The deconvolution of C 1 s spectrum presents three peaks, assignable to sp3 C-C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.2 eV), sp2 CO (288.4 eV), and OC-O (289.7 eV), respectively, from intrinsic interlayer CO3 2- anions (Fig. 5d). By contrast, the locations of Co 2p and Al 2p of B/CAL-2 exhibit distinct shifts towards higher values of binding energy, while the location of C1s also changes with the deconvoluted peaks of C-C/CC (285.0 eV), C-O (286.3 eV), and CO (288.4 eV), respectively. A minor deconvoluted peak of C-N (287.1 eV) is found possibly due to the introduction of organic nitrogen from magnolia blossoms. These variations imply the interaction of interspersed B-CQDs with Co/Al cations. Fig. 5e shows the FTIR spectra of different samples. The B-CQDs have versatile functional groups, mainly including sp2 CC at 1470 cm−1, C-O stretching vibration at 1139 cm−1, and O-H bending vibration at 1018 cm−1. For the CAL, the broad peak at 3510 cm−1 is related to O-H stretching vibration. The peak at 1648 cm−1 corresponds to the bending mode of water molecules. Two strong peaks at 1362 cm−1 and 756 cm−1 belong to the vibrational and bending modes of interlayer CO3 2−, respectively [34]. In addition, the peaks at 576 cm−1 and 443 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching and bending vibrations of Co-O and Al-O of the hydrotalcite materials [35]. After integration with the B-CQDs, the FTIR spectra of resulting B/CAL-2 well inherit those of the CAL with relatively weak peak intensities, implying that the introduction of B-CQDs does not alter the skeleton structure of CoAl-LDH only with some interaction.

Fig. 6a records the UV–vis DRS spectra of CAL and B/CAL-2. Two samples show two representative absorption spectra. One is a broad band in the visible region centered at ∼525 nm from the 4 T1 g (F) → 4 T1 g (P) transitions of octahedral Co2+ within the CoAl-LDH layer. The other is in the UV region below 300 nm from ligand to metal charge transfer at the surface of CoAl-LDH layer [36]. Both lead to the broader absorption of visible light. In the inset of Fig. 6, two typical absorption peaks, attributable to the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 hybridized conjugated CC bond and the n-π * transitions from the surface oxygen-containing functional groups, can be found at 230 and 310 nm, respectively. With the characteristic peak of octahedral Co2+ transition, the introduction of B-CQDs greatly promotes light adsorption capacity. The band gap (Eg) of CAL and B/CAL-2 calculated from the conventional Tauc equation are 2.14 and 2.16 eV, respectively (Fig. 6b), indicating that the interspersed B-CQDs have no effect on the Eg of B/CAL-2. Fig. 6c depicts the XPS valence band (VB) spectrum of CAL. By making tangent lines, the VB position (EV) is determined to be 1. 29 eV vs. NHE. Therefore, the conduction band (CB) position (EC) is calculated to be − 0.87 eV vs. NHE by the classic equation of EC = EV – Eg, which is enough negative in theory to reduce CO2 into various C1 fuels. In addition, the up-converted PL spectra of B-CQDs are measured with excitation wavelengths from 650 to 900 nm. As shown in Fig. 6d, the PL intensity is excitation wavelength-dependent. Approaching to near-infrared light irradiation, the B-CQDs emit obvious visible light between 350 and 600 nm with the strongest emitting light at 406 nm under an excitation wavelength of 700 nm. The multiphoton activation from the nonconstant energy relationship between the excitation and emission of light during the upconversion process is supposed to explain this phenomenon [37,38]. Evidently, such up-converted B-CQDs are favorable to convert more near-infrared to visible light and further enhance the photocatalytic performance of CAL potentially. Transient photocurrents and EIS are further investigated to monitor the migration and separation behaviors of photogenerated charge carriers. As shown in Fig. 6e, three samples display different photocurrent responses under an on-and-off cycle mode of light irradiation. The B/CAL-2 exhibits the largest photocurrent, which is nearly 3-, and 6- times higher than those of CAL and B-CQDs, respectively, moreover, the B/CAL-2 has the smallest fitted diameter (Fig. 6f), well evidencing the lowest charge-transfer resistance and recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The photocatalytic activities of samples for CO2 reduction are evaluated without addition of any sacrificial and photosensitizer under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation at room temperature (∼23 °C). Two gas-phase products from CO2 reduction, CO and CH4, are detected. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, all the samples show proportional increase of products to the irradiation time. The CAL has the poorest CO and CH4 yields. With the aid of B-CQDs, the resulting B/CALs exhibit significantly enhanced product yields high up to 20.5 μmol·g−1 of CO and 11.3 μmol·g−1 of CH4 for the optimal sample of B/CAL-2, 6.4- and 7.5-fold higher than those of the CAL, respectively. The converted evolution rates of the two products are 4.1 and 2.3 μmol·g−1·h−1, respectively, surpassing many reported high-level LDHs counterparts (Table S1). Meanwhile, O2 from the competitive reaction of splitting water is also found over the B/CAL-2 with a yield of 47.6 μmol·g−1. The stoichiometric ratio of total CO and CH4 to O2, in terms of the yield rate, is calculated to be ∼0.67 (
20.5 + 11.3
47.6
), close to the theoretical value of 0.8. Accordingly, the detected yield of H2 evolution is as low as to 6.8 μmol·g−1 (Fig. 7c), well confirming that most of the photogenerated electrons from the valence band (VB, 5 H2O → 5/2 O2 + 10 H+ + 10 e-) of the B/CAL-2 are involved in the reaction for the reduction of CO2 but not for the reduction of H2O. Indeed, the mechanism of CO2 reduction for a heterogeneous photocatalyst is always very complicated, and the excitation, transfer of electrons, and changes of intermediate product(s) might experience some other unpredictable procedure. Addition of low amount or excessive of B-CQDs (B/CAL-1 and B/CAL-3) leads to the striking activity deterioration, arising presumedly from the relatively weak electron transfer capacity on the interface of CAL and the overwhelming coverage of B-CQDs to CAL with insufficient light absorption and utilization induced by the B-CQDs, respectively. In addition, CO and CH4 are not detected under several given reference evaluations, such as reaction with Ar atmosphere only, in absence of light irradiation, a photocatalyst, CO2 or H2O, excluding the origin of CO2 from the thermal reduction itself or some organic contaminants absorbed on the photocatalyst surface. Because both B-CQDs and CAL contain carbon element, which can act as potential carbon sources to contribute to the total CO2 and CH4, the 13C isotopic tracing experiment is tested for the B/CAL-2 with 13CO2 as the feedstock to confirm the carbon origin in the reaction, as shown in Fig. 7d. Combining with a mass spectrometer, the 13C and 16O fragments, 13CH4, 13CO, 16O2, and 13CO2 are observed, indicating that CO2 and CH4 are indeed the products of CO2 reduction with H2O. Besides, we conduct an ion-exchange operation for the B/CAL-2 with a 0.5 M of NaH2PO4 aqueous solution to remove the interlayer CO3 2− anions in the CAL as much as possible. With the identical activity evaluation as the untreated B/CAL-2, the CO and CH4 yields are 19.0 and 10.2 μmol·g−1, 92.7% and 90.3% of their total yields, respectively, suggesting the predominant role of CO2 photoreduction. In order to check if there is a photothermal effect, we perform the photocatalytic activity evaluation of B/CAL-2 under region of near-infrared to infrared light (wavelengths from 800 nm to 2000 nm), where the photothermal effect could be induced by B-CQDs. The result shows that no any CO evolution is detected, excluding the possibility of photothermal effect of B-CQDs on the CO2 photoreduction.

Based on the experimental results, an uncomplicated mechanism on CO2 photoreduction for the B/CAL-2 is proposed graphically, as demonstrated in Fig. 8. Through the optical and photoelectrochemical measurements, the EC and EV are − 0.87 and 1.29 eV, respectively, which well meet the theoretical potential requirements for the formation of CO (−0.53 eV) and CH4 (−0.24 eV). When the B/CAL-2 is exposed by light source, its constitutive CoAl-LDH will absorb visible light and generate electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated electrons then migrate from the VB to the CB of CoAl-LDH, leaving holes in its VB. Subsequently, they transfer rapidly to the B-CQDs because of the positive synergistic effect between B-CQDs and CoAl-LDH, leading to a higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the B/CAL-2. The adsorbed CO2 molecules react with protons (H+) from the simultaneous oxidation of H2O molecules and the accumulated photogenerated electrons on B-CQDs, so as to produce CO and CH4. Overall, the possible reaction formulas are listed as follows:
(1)
CoAl − LDH + h υ → CoAl − LDH

)

)

eV )

Instead of traditional CQDs from chemical synthesis, renewable biomass-derived carbon quantum dots interspersed CoAl-LDH photocatalysts (B/CALs) are successfully prepared by means of a simple hydrothermal method. The introduction of B-CQDs promotes light absorption and increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. With strong interaction between the highly-dispersed B-CQDs and CoAl-LDH, the optimal sample of B/CAL-2 with double SSA and an appropriate content of B-CQDs, exhibits significantly enhanced photoreduction activity of CO2-to-CO and CH4, 6.4- and 7.5-fold higher than those of the CAL, respectively, without addition of any sacrificial reagent. This work provides a green, low-cost, and waste profitable idea to prepare CQDs-modified semiconductor photocatalysts for efficient reduction of CO2.

Jia Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing − original draft. Ge Gao, Yang Liu, Yang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Zhi Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly acknowledge the financial support to this work through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871124), the Start-up grant under 130–09419024 from Shantou University (Shantou, China), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (2021A1515010071), and Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry (Jilin Normal University), Ministry of Education of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jcou.2022.102257.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
